title: Keypad Calculator
subtitle: Press numbers and + − × ÷ → show result on LCD!
overview: Students build a working calculator using a 4x4 keypad and 16x2 LCD display connected to an Arduino. By pressing number keys and operator keys, the result is computed and shown live on the LCD — combining hardware wiring, library usage, and logic programming.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — keypad wiring, LCD display, Arduino setup, student experiments.

gallery.caption: 4×4 Keypad — 16 buttons for numbers and operators
16×2 LCD Display — Shows 16 characters across 2 rows

gallery.caption: I2C Module — Reduces LCD wiring from 16 pins to just 4!
Arduino UNO — The brain that runs the calculator logic

gallery.caption: Live demo: pressing 3 + 5 = shows 8 on LCD!

keyTopics: 4×4 Keypad — 16 keys wired in a row-column matrix; Arduino scans to detect which key is pressed
16×2 LCD Display — Shows 32 characters total; used with I2C module for easy 4-wire connection
I2C Protocol — Lets Arduino talk to LCD using only 2 wires (SDA + SCL) instead of 8
Keypad Library — keypad.getKey() reads which button is pressed
LiquidCrystal_I2C Library — lcd.print() sends text to the display
String Concatenation — Building a number digit by digit as user presses keys
Arithmetic Operations — Performing +, −, ×, ÷ based on operator key pressed
Input Parsing — Splitting the entered expression into two numbers and an operator
Edge Cases — Handling division by zero, decimal results, and clearing with # key

activity.title: Keypad + LCD Calculator
steps: Connect the I2C module to the LCD display (comes pre-soldered on most modules)
Wire LCD I2C to Arduino: VCC→5V, GND→GND, SDA→A4, SCL→A5
Connect 4×4 keypad row pins to Arduino digital pins 9, 8, 7, 6
Connect 4×4 keypad column pins to Arduino digital pins 5, 4, 3, 2
Install Keypad and LiquidCrystal_I2C libraries from Arduino Library Manager
Upload the calculator code to Arduino
Press number keys → operator (* / + -) → number → # to see result on LCD!
Challenge: Add a memory button to store the last result!

quiz.q: How many buttons does a 4×4 keypad have?
quiz.explanation: A 4×4 keypad has 4 rows × 4 columns = 16 buttons total — numbers 0–9, letters A–D, *, #, and operators.

quiz.q: How does the Arduino know which key on the keypad is pressed?
options: Each key has its own dedicated wire to Arduino
It scans rows and columns in a grid pattern to detect which key is pressed
The keypad sends a Bluetooth signal
It uses a camera to read the key
quiz.explanation: Keypad uses a ROW-COLUMN matrix. Arduino sends signals through rows and reads columns to figure out which key is pressed — saves pins!

quiz.q: What does the I2C module do for the LCD?
options: Reduces the number of wires needed from 16 to just 4
quiz.explanation: I2C module is a clever adapter — instead of 8+ wires for a normal LCD, you only need VCC, GND, SDA, SCL — just 4 wires!

quiz.q: Which Arduino pins are used for I2C communication?
quiz.explanation: On Arduino UNO, I2C uses A4 for SDA (data) and A5 for SCL (clock). These are the dedicated I2C pins!

quiz.q: In the calculator code, what does keypad.getKey() do?
options: Reads which key on the keypad was most recently pressed
quiz.explanation: keypad.getKey() checks the keypad and returns the character of whichever key is currently being pressed — or nothing if no key is pressed.

quiz.q: How many characters can a 16×2 LCD display show at once?
quiz.explanation: 16×2 means 16 columns × 2 rows = 32 characters total displayed at once. Row 1 shows the input, Row 2 shows the result!

quiz.q: In the calculator, which key is used to calculate and show the result?
quiz.explanation: The # key acts as the EQUALS button! When pressed, Arduino evaluates the expression and shows the result on the LCD.

quiz.q: What happens if you divide a number by zero on the calculator?
options: The code should detect it and show an error message like 'Error!'
The number gets very big and wraps around
quiz.explanation: Good code checks for division by zero and prints 'Error!' on the LCD instead of crashing — this is called edge case handling!

quiz.q: Which library is used to control the LCD display?
quiz.explanation: LiquidCrystal_I2C library provides easy commands like lcd.print(), lcd.clear(), lcd.setCursor() to control the LCD over I2C.

quiz.q: What is String concatenation used for in the calculator project?
options: To connect the LCD to Arduino
To build a multi-digit number as the user presses keys one by one
To control the brightness of the LCD
quiz.explanation: When you press '1', '2', '3' separately, the code concatenates them into the String '123' — then converts to a number for calculation!

quiz.q: Which command moves the LCD cursor to the second row?
quiz.explanation: lcd.setCursor(column, row) positions the cursor. lcd.setCursor(0, 1) moves to column 0, row 1 (second row — rows start at 0)!

quiz.q: What does the * key represent in this calculator?
quiz.explanation: On the keypad, * is used as the MULTIPLY operator. The code checks if the pressed key is '*' and stores multiplication as the operation.

quiz.q: What is the purpose of lcd.clear() in the code?
options: Wipes all text from the LCD screen — used before showing a new result
quiz.explanation: lcd.clear() clears all 32 characters on the display. We use it before showing the result so old text doesn't mix with new text!

quiz.q: In a row-column keypad matrix, how many Arduino pins does a 4×4 keypad use?
quiz.explanation: Smart matrix design! 4 rows + 4 columns = only 8 Arduino pins needed to handle 16 buttons. Much better than 16 separate wires!

quiz.q: What challenge can students try after the basic calculator works?
options: Add a memory button to store the last result
quiz.explanation: A great extension challenge is adding a memory feature — press a key to store the last result and use it in the next calculation!
